body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd
import pyarrow as pa
import platform

print("=== VERSIONS ===")
print(f"Python:   {platform.python_version()}")
print(f"Pandas:   {pd.__version__}")
print(f"PyArrow:  {pa.__version__}")

base1 = pd.DataFrame({"a": ["short"] * 10, "b": ["x"] * 10})
base2 = pd.DataFrame({"a": ["short"] * 10, "b": ["x"] * 10})
base1["id"] = range(10)
base2["id"] = range(10, 20)

new_row = pd.DataFrame({"a": ["NEW_ROW"], "b": ["x"], "id": [-1]})
split_idx = 5

print()
print("=== 3-WAY CONCAT + NULL ASSIGNMENT (buggy) ===")
df_3way = pd.concat([base1, base2], ignore_index=True)
df_3way = pd.concat([
    df_3way.iloc[:split_idx],
    new_row,
    df_3way.iloc[split_idx:]
], ignore_index=True)
df_3way.loc[[0], "a"] = pd.NA
print(df_3way.to_string())

print()
print("=== 3-WAY CONCAT + STRING ASSIGNMENT (works fine) ===")
df_3way_str = pd.concat([base1, base2], ignore_index=True)
df_3way_str = pd.concat([
    df_3way_str.iloc[:split_idx],
    new_row,
    df_3way_str.iloc[split_idx:]
], ignore_index=True)
df_3way_str.loc[[0], "a"] = "something"
print(df_3way_str.to_string())

print()
print("=== 2-WAY CONCAT + NULL ASSIGNMENT (safe workaround) ===")
df_2way = pd.concat([base1, base2], ignore_index=True)
df_2way["sort"] = range(len(df_2way))
new_row2 = pd.DataFrame({"a": ["NEW_ROW"], "b": ["x"], "id": [-1], "sort": [split_idx + 0.5]})
df_2way = (
    pd.concat([df_2way, new_row2], ignore_index=True)
    .sort_values("sort")
    .reset_index(drop=True)
)
df_2way.loc[[0], "a"] = pd.NA
print(df_2way.to_string())
```

### Issue Description

When inserting a row at a specific position using a 3-way slice concat, subsequent assignment of `pd.NA` to a string column silently corrupts some rows.

In the buggy case, rows 6–10 (ids 5–9) have their `a` value silently wiped to empty string despite not being part of the assignment. The `b` and `id` columns in those same rows are completely unaffected, confirming the corruption is isolated to the Arrow string column.

Two comparisons are included to narrow down the cause:

- **3-way concat + regular string assignment** works correctly, showing the bug is specific to null assignment
- **2-way concat + null assignment** works correctly, showing the bug is specific to the 3-chunk structure created by the slice

No exception is raised — the corruption is completely silent, making it particularly dangerous in production code.

This may be related to #64320 but distinct in that the corruption manifests as silent data loss rather than an exception, and is triggered by a 3-chunk structure rather than a 2-chunk structure.

Here is the output of the minimal test:

title: DEPR: deprecate set_eng_float_format
labels: [{'id': 13101118, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwxMzEwMTExOA==', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/labels/Output-Formatting', 'name': 'Output-Formatting', 'color': 'ededed', 'default': False, 'description': '__repr__ of pandas objects, to_string'}, {'id': 87485152, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw4NzQ4NTE1Mg==', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/labels/Deprecate', 'name': 'Deprecate', 'color': '5319e7', 'default': False, 'description': 'Functionality to remove in pandas'}, {'id': 219960758, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyMTk5NjA3NTg=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/labels/Needs%20Discussion', 'name': 'Needs Discussion', 'color': '207de5', 'default': False, 'description': 'Requires discussion from core team before further action'}]
body: Claude wrote this at my request. ## Proposal Deprecate `pd.set_eng_float_format()` in favor of using `pd.set_option("display.float_format", EngFormatter(...))` directly. Human Update Or just deprecate without an alternative. I haven't seen any evidence that anyone uses this. ## Rationale `set_eng_float_format` is a legacy convenience function (added in v0.5.0) that is a thin wrapper around `set_option`: ```python def set_eng_float_format(accuracy=3, use_eng_prefix=False): set_option("display.float_format", EngFormatter(accuracy, use_eng_prefix)) ``` It is the only top-level `pd.set_*` function that exists outside the options API, making it an odd outlier in the public API. This was already identified in #15610, where the consensus was to integrate this into the options machinery or deprecate it. **Evidence of minimal usage:** - **GitHub code search** yields ~30 results for `set_eng_float_format` outside pandas-dev/pandas. The vast majority are vendored copies of pandas, type stubs, or re-exports (xorbits, modin, Apache Beam, pyparallel, pandas-stubs, etc.). Only **2** results are actual user code calling this function. - **Stack Overflow** has **zero** questions or answers mentioning `set_eng_float_format`. - **Only 2 bug reports** have ever been filed against this function in the pandas issue tracker (#11981 in 2016, #15610 in 2017). The replacement is straightforward and equally concise: ```python # Before pd.set_eng_float_format(accuracy=3, use_eng_prefix=True) # After from pandas.io.formats.format import EngFormatter pd.set_option("display.float_format", EngFormatter(accuracy=3, use_eng_prefix=True)) ``` This keeps `EngFormatter` available (it is not being deprecated), removes a one-off top-level function that doesn't fit the options API pattern, and simplifies the public API surface. Closes #15610.

title: DOC: Fix incorrect Sphinx cross references in older, whatsnew restructred text files.
labels: [{'id': 134699, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwxMzQ2OTk=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/labels/Docs', 'name': 'Docs', 'color': '3465A4', 'default': False, 'description': None}]
body:
The top level `doc/` directory contains [reStructuredText](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/restructuredtext/basics.html#rst-primer) files who's names will end in `.rst` and are used to build the pandas documentation using [Sphinx](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/index.html). These files can contain Sphinx [cross-referencing](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/referencing.html) syntax to create a link to where the reference is documented. Specifically, the [Python cross-referencing syntax](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/domains/python.html#python-xref-roles) is used. Cross references should reference currently documented pandas objects or objects in other libaries specified in the `intersphinx_mapping` variable in `doc/source/conf.py`

When `sphinx-build` command is run with [`nitpicky = True`](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/configuration.html#confval-nitpicky) set in the `doc/source/conf.py`, the logs will specify the file location, line number, and invalid cross references. title: DOC: Public APIs missing from the API reference documentation
labels: [{'id': 134699, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwxMzQ2OTk=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/labels/Docs', 'name': 'Docs', 'color': '3465A4', 'default': False, 'description': None}, {'id': 1954720290, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwxOTU0NzIwMjkw', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/labels/Needs%20Triage', 'name': 'Needs Triage', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False, 'description': 'Issue that has not been reviewed by a pandas team member'}]
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that the issue still exists on the latest versions of the docs on `main` [here](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/)


### Location of the documentation

https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/reference/index.html

### Documentation problem

The following 5 public APIs have docstrings in the source code but are not listed
in any `doc/source/reference/*.rst` file, so they do not appear in the rendered
API reference.

Found by comparing `dir()` output on core classes against the autosummary entries
parsed from `doc/source/reference/*.rst`, then filtering out aliases (e.g.
`divide`→`div`, `tolist`→`to_list`) and methods already documented via a
property or on a parent/sibling class (e.g. `Index.transpose` via `Index.T`).

### Series (2 items)

| API | Source | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `pandas.Series.divmod` | `pandas/core/series.py` | Element-wise integer division and modulo (binary operator `divmod`) |
| `pandas.Series.rdivmod` | `pandas/core/series.py` | Reverse integer division and modulo (binary operator `rdivmod`) |

Note: every other binary operator pair (`div`/`rdiv`, `mod`/`rmod`, `add`/`radd`,
etc.) is listed in the "Binary operator functions" section of `series.rst`.
`divmod` and `rdivmod` are the only pair missing.

### Timestamp (2 items)

| API | Source | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `pandas.Timestamp.fromisocalendar` | C extension (`_libs.tslibs.timestamps`) | Constructs a Timestamp from ISO year, week number, and weekday |
| `pandas.Timestamp.fromisoformat` | C extension (`_libs.tslibs.timestamps`) | Constructs a Timestamp from an ISO 8601 format string |

These are pandas-specific overrides of `datetime.datetime` class methods. Other
Timestamp constructors (`Timestamp.now`, `Timestamp.today`, `Timestamp.combine`,
etc.) are already documented in `indexing.rst`.

### Timedelta (1 item)

| API | Source | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `pandas.Timedelta.resolution_string` | `pandas/_libs/tslibs/timedeltas.pyx` | Returns a string representing the lowest timedelta resolution |

Other Timedelta properties (`components`, `days`, `seconds`, `nanoseconds`,
`resolution_string`'s companion `resolution`, etc.) are already listed in
`indexing.rst`.


### Suggested fix for documentation

1. Add `Series.divmod` and `Series.rdivmod` to `doc/source/reference/series.rst`
   under the "Binary operator functions" subsection, alongside `div`, `rdiv`,
   `mod`, `rmod`, etc.

2. Add `Timestamp.fromisocalendar` and `Timestamp.fromisoformat` to
   `doc/source/reference/indexing.rst` under the Timestamp section, alongside
   the existing constructors (`Timestamp.now`, `Timestamp.today`, etc.).

### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [ ] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

x = pd.DataFrame(
    np.arange(30).reshape(5, 6),
    columns=pd.MultiIndex.from_product((list("XY"), list("ABC"))),
)
y = pd.DataFrame(np.arange(15).reshape(5, 3), columns=list("ABC"))
print(x.div(y, axis=1, level=1))
```

### Issue Description

Pandas v3.0.1 gives the below output, seemingly ignoring the `axis` kwarg:

```

    X           Y        
    A   B   C   A   B   C
0 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
1 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
2 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
3 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
4 NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN
```

In Pandas v2.3.2 instead this was the (as expected) output:

```

     X                             Y                    
     A         B         C         A         B         C
0  NaN  1.000000  1.000000       inf  4.000000  2.500000
1  2.0  1.750000  1.600000  3.000000  2.500000  2.200000
2  2.0  1.857143  1.750000  2.500000  2.285714  2.125000
3  2.0  1.900000  1.818182  2.333333  2.200000  2.090909
4  2.0  1.923077  1.857143  2.250000  2.153846  2.071429
```

### Expected Behavior

```

     X                             Y                    
     A         B         C         A         B         C
0  NaN  1.000000  1.000000       inf  4.000000  2.500000
1  2.0  1.750000  1.600000  3.000000  2.500000  2.200000
2  2.0  1.857143  1.750000  2.500000  2.285714  2.125000
3  2.0  1.900000  1.818182  2.333333  2.200000  2.090909
4  2.0  1.923077  1.857143  2.250000  2.153846  2.071429
```

title: Double braces used in docs in `read_csv`
body:
**This is not sufficient.**

The issue appears in multiple places across the documentation (e.g., parameter descriptions and examples where {{ }} is rendered instead of { }). We should identify and fix the root cause or update all affected occurrences, otherwise the docs will remain inconsistent and continue to show invalid Python syntax in other sections.

Just in `reference/api/pandas.read_csv.html` ([link](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.read_csv.html)), there are at least:
- Double braces `{{ }}` appear in code examples instead of normal Python dict braces `{ }`, producing invalid syntax (e.g., `dtype={{"Value": float}}`).

- The `date_format` example also uses double braces:  
  `date_format={{"col 2": "%d/%m/%Y", "col 3": "%a %d %b %Y"}}`.

- Parameter type description for `dtype` renders incorrectly as:  
  `dict of {{Hashable dtype}}`.

- Parameter type description for `converters` renders incorrectly as:  
  `dict of {{HashableCallable}}`.

- Parameter type description for `na_values` renders incorrectly as:  
  `dict of {{HashableIterable}}`.

- Enum values for `engine` render incorrectly as:  
  `engine {{‘c’, ‘python’, ‘pyarrow’}}`.

- Enum values for `quoting` render incorrectly as:  
  `{{0 or csv.QUOTE_MINIMAL, 1 or csv.QUOTE_ALL, ...}}`.

- Header renaming description renders placeholders incorrectly as:  
  `".{{count}}"`.

- Placeholder examples for unnamed columns render incorrectly as:  
  `"Unnamed: {{i}}"` and `"Unnamed: {{i}}_level_{{level}}"`.

- Several dictionary examples throughout the page use `{{ }}` instead of `{ }`, which makes the shown Python code invalid.

_Originally posted by @smith558 in https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/64412#pullrequestreview-3902956455_

title: DOC: rendering bug - `{{ }}` shown instead of `{ }` across API documentation
labels: [{'id': 134699, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwxMzQ2OTk=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/labels/Docs', 'name': 'Docs', 'color': '3465A4', 'default': False, 'description': None}]
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that the issue still exists on the latest versions of the docs on `main` [here](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/)


### Location of the documentation

https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.read_csv.html
https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.read_sas.html
https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.read_table.html
... **seemingly all pages**

### Documentation problem

The issue appears in multiple places across the documentation (e.g., parameter descriptions and examples where `{{ }}` is rendered instead of `{ }`). We should identify and fix the root cause or update all affected occurrences; otherwise, the docs will remain inconsistent and continue to show invalid Python syntax in other sections.

Related to https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/64394.

Just in `reference/api/pandas.read_csv.html` ([link](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.read_csv.html)), there are at least:
- Double braces `{{ }}` appear in code examples instead of normal Python dict braces `{ }`, producing invalid syntax (e.g., `dtype={{"Value": float}}`).

- The `date_format` example also uses double braces:  
  `date_format={{"col 2": "%d/%m/%Y", "col 3": "%a %d %b %Y"}}`.

- Parameter type description for `dtype` renders incorrectly as:  
  `dict of {{Hashable dtype}}`.

- Parameter type description for `converters` renders incorrectly as:  
  `dict of {{HashableCallable}}`.

- Parameter type description for `na_values` renders incorrectly as:  
  `dict of {{HashableIterable}}`.

- Enum values for `engine` render incorrectly as:  
  `engine {{‘c’, ‘python’, ‘pyarrow’}}`.

- Enum values for `quoting` render incorrectly as:  
  `{{0 or csv.QUOTE_MINIMAL, 1 or csv.QUOTE_ALL, ...}}`.

- Header renaming description renders placeholders incorrectly as:  
  `".{{count}}"`.

- Placeholder examples for unnamed columns render incorrectly as:  
  `"Unnamed: {{i}}"` and `"Unnamed: {{i}}_level_{{level}}"`.

- Several dictionary examples throughout the page use `{{ }}` instead of `{ }`, which makes the shown Python code invalid.

### Suggested fix for documentation

I assume the double braces were originally used to escape literal `{}` in docstrings that passed through a .format()/Substitution step. Since the formatting step is seemingly no longer applied (TBC?) in the current doc build, the escapes now leak into the rendered docs, so they should be replaced across the repo with normal braces only if we can confirm they're no longer necessary or we need to fix the build step.
